title: Alternating series remainder
description: Apply the alternating series remainder to approximate alternating series to a given error bound.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{(-1)}^{n+1}}}{{{n}^{3/2}}}}$

**Using the alternating series error test, what is the least number of terms necessary to approximate the series to within $\pm 0.001$ of the actual value?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
According to the alternating series test, the error bound is given by the first omitted  term in the series.  Hence, we want to find the smallest integer $~k~$ such that $~\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^{3/2}}\le0.001\,$.  

(Note that we can focus on the size of the error and hence ignore whether it is a negative or positive.)

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^{3/2}}&\le 0.001
\\\\
(k+1)^{3/2}&\ge 1000
\\\\
k+1&\ge 100
\\\\
k&\ge 99
\end{align}$

Hence, $99$ is the least number of terms that will give the desired accuracy.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }~{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}$

**Using the alternating series error test, what is the least number of terms necessary to approximate the series to within $\pm 0.001$ of the actual value?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
According to the alternating series test, the error bound is given by the first omitted  term of the series.  Hence, we want to find the smallest integer $~k~$ such that $~\displaystyle\frac{1}{k+1}\le 0.001\,$.  

(Note that we can focus on the size of the error and hence ignore whether it is positive or negative.)

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\frac{1}{k+1}&\le0.001
\\\\
k+1&\ge 1000
\\\\
k&\ge 999
\end{align}$

Hence, $999$ is the least number of terms that will give the desired accuracy.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n^3}}$

**Using the alternating series error test, what is the least number of terms necessary to approximate the series to within $\pm 0.001$ of the actual value?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
According to the alternating series test, the error bound is given by the first omitted  term in the series.  Hence, we want to find the smallest integer $~k~$ such that $~\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^3}\le0.001\,$.  

(Note that we can focus on the size of the error and hence ignore whether it is positive or negative.)

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^3}&\le 0.001
\\\\
(k+1)^3&\ge 1000
\\\\
k+1&\ge 10
\\\\
k&\ge 9
\end{align}$

Hence, $9$ is the least number of terms that will give the desired accuracy.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n^2}}$

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
According to the alternating series test, the error bound is given by the first omitted  term in the series.  Hence, we want to find the smallest integer $~k~$ such that $~\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^2}\le0.001\,$.  

(Note that we can focus on the size of the error and hence ignore whether it is a positive or negative.)

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^2}&\le 0.001

\\\\

Hence, $31$ is the least number of terms that will give the desired accuracy.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n^4}}$

**Using the alternating series error test, what is the least number of terms necessary to approximate the series to within $\pm 0.0001$ of the actual value?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
According to the alternating series test, the error bound is given by the first omitted term in the series.  Hence, we want to find the smallest integer $~k~$ such that $~\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^4}\le0.0001\,$.  

(Note that we can focus on the size of the error and hence ignore whether it is a negative or positive.)

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\frac{1}{(k+1)^4}&\le 0.0001
\\\\
(k+1)^4&\ge 10,000
\\\\
k+1&\ge 10
\\\\
k&\ge 9
\end{align}$

Hence, $9$ is the least number of terms that will give the desired accuracy.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{(-1)}^{n+1}}}{{\sqrt{n}}}}$

**Using the alternating series error test, what is the least number of terms necessary to approximate the series to within $\pm 0.001$ of the actual value?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
According to the alternating series test, the error bound is given by the first omitted term in the series.  Hence, we want to find the smallest integer $~k~$ such that $~\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{k+1}}\le0.001~$.  

(Note that we can focus on the size of the error and hence ignore whether it is positive or negative.)

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{k+1}}&\le 0.001
\\\\
\sqrt{k+1}&\ge 1000
\\\\
k+1&\ge 1{,}000{,}000
\\\\
k&\ge 999{,}999
\end{align}$

Hence, $999{,}999$ is the least number of terms that will give the desired accuracy.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}$ is approximated using the partial sum $\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{100}{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}$.

**Complete the sentence about the error of the approximation.**

The error bound is 
[[☃ dropdown 1]] and hence the approximation is an [[☃ dropdown 2]].

**What is the absolute value of the error bound?**  
*Round to four decimal places.*

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: The alternating series error test tells us that the error bound is given by the first omitted term in the series.  In this case, that means the error bound is $~\displaystyle\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{102}}}{101} \approx0.0099\,$.
Since the error bound is a positive number, the approximation must be less than the actual value.  Hence, the partial sum is an underestimate.

In conclusion, The error bound is 
*positive* and hence the approximation is an *underestimate*.

The absolute value of the error bound, rounded to four decimal places, is $0.0099$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{(2n)!}}$ is approximated using the partial sum $\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{3}{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{(2n)!}}$.

**Complete the sentence about the error of the approximation.**

The error bound is 
[[☃ dropdown 1]] and hence the approximation is an [[☃ dropdown 2]].

**What is the absolute value of the error bound?**  
*Round to five decimal places.*

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: The alternating series error test tells that the error bound is given by the first omitted term of the series.  In this case, that means the error bound is $~\displaystyle\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{5}}}{8!} \approx-0.00002$.
Since the error bound is a negative number, the approximation must be more than the actual value.  Hence, the partial sum is an overestimate.

In conclusion, The error bound is 
*negative* and hence the approximation is an *overestimate*.

The absolute value of the error bound, rounded to five decimal places, is $0.00002$.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty }~{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}$ is approximated using the partial sum $\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=1}^{99}~{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}}{n}}$.

**Complete the sentence about the error of the approximation.**

The error bound is 
[[☃ dropdown 1]] and hence the approximation is an [[☃ dropdown 2]].

**What is the absolute value of the error bound?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints: The alternating series error test tells that the error is approximated by the first omitted term in the series.  In this case, that means the error is between $~0~$ and  $~\displaystyle\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{101}}}{100} =-0.01\,$.
Since the error estimate is a negative number, the approximation must be larger than the actual value.  Hence, the partial sum is an overestimate.

In conclusion, The error bound is 
*negative* and hence the approximation is an *overestimate*.

The absolute value of the error bound is $0.01$.
